subject: ap-chemistry
title: Intermolecular Forces & Physical Properties
stimulus:
The table above gives the molecular structures and boiling points for the compounds CS
and COS.

parts.prompt:
In terms of the types and relative strengths of all the intermolecular forces in each compound, explain why
the boiling point of CS (l) is higher than that of COS(l).
parts.scoring_notes: CS$_2$ is a linear symmetric molecule (S=C=S) — it is nonpolar (no net dipole). COS is a linear asymmetric molecule (O=C=S) — it is polar (C=O and C=S dipoles do not cancel). However, CS$_2$ boils at 319 K while COS boils at 223 K. CS$_2$ has only London dispersion forces (LDFs). COS has dipole–dipole forces AND London dispersion forces. Despite having additional dipole–dipole forces, COS boils lower than CS$_2$. The reason: CS$_2$ has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud (2 S atoms with 16 electrons each vs. 1 S and 1 O). More electrons → stronger LDFs in CS$_2$. The stronger LDFs in CS$_2$ outweigh the dipole–dipole forces in COS → CS$_2$ has a higher boiling point.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying the IMF types in each compound: 1 point

required_elements: CS$_2$: only London dispersion forces (LDFs) — nonpolar molecule.
COS: dipole–dipole forces AND London dispersion forces — polar molecule.
alternatives.correct_example: CS$_2$ (S=C=S) is linear and symmetric → nonpolar → only London dispersion forces. COS (O=C=S) is linear but asymmetric → polar (net dipole) → dipole–dipole forces AND London dispersion forces.

wrong_examples: Saying CS$_2$ has dipole–dipole forces — CS$_2$ is nonpolar (symmetric), so no net dipole and no dipole–dipole forces.
Saying COS has hydrogen bonding — COS has no O–H, N–H, or F–H bonds required for H-bonding.
common_traps: Linear symmetric molecule (CS$_2$): equal and opposite bond dipoles cancel → net dipole = 0 → nonpolar → only LDFs. Linear asymmetric molecule (COS): different atoms on each side → net dipole ≠ 0 → polar.

scoring_points.description: For explaining that stronger LDFs in CS$_2$ (more/larger electron cloud) outweigh dipole–dipole in COS: 1 point

required_elements: CS$_2$ has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud (2 S atoms, larger and more electrons) than COS (1 S + 1 O).
The stronger LDFs in CS$_2$ exceed the combined IMFs in COS → CS$_2$ has a higher boiling point.
alternatives.correct_example: CS$_2$ has 2 large, highly polarizable S atoms (16 electrons each) compared to COS (1 S + 1 O with 8 electrons). More electrons and larger atoms → stronger London dispersion forces. The LDFs in CS$_2$ are stronger than the combined LDF + dipole–dipole forces in COS. Therefore CS$_2$ requires more energy to vaporize → higher boiling point.

wrong_examples: Saying CS$_2$ is higher because it has dipole–dipole forces — CS$_2$ is nonpolar; the higher BP comes from stronger LDFs, not dipole–dipole.
Saying dipole–dipole forces always dominate LDFs — for molecules of very different polarizability, LDFs can exceed dipole–dipole.
common_traps: LDF strength increases with electron count and molecular size (polarizability). A large nonpolar molecule can have stronger LDFs than a small polar molecule, leading to a higher BP.

parts.prompt:
A 10.0 g sample of CS
(l) is put in an evacuated 5.0 L rigid container. The container is sealed and heated to
325 K, at which temperature all of the CS
(l) has vaporized. What is the pressure in the container once all of
the CS
(l) has vaporized?
HF(aq) + $H_2O$(l) F − (aq) + $H_2O$+(aq)
2 3
parts.scoring_notes: CS$_2$ molar mass = 12.01 + 2(32.07) = 76.15 g/mol. Moles CS$_2$ = 10.0 g / 76.15 g/mol = 0.1313 mol. Use ideal gas law: PV = nRT. P = nRT/V = (0.1313 mol)(0.08206 L·atm/mol·K)(325 K) / (5.0 L) = 3.504 / 5.0 = 0.70 atm.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of CS$_2$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using molar mass of CO$_2$ (44.01 g/mol) or another compound instead of CS$_2$ (76.15 g/mol).
common_traps: CS$_2$ = C + 2S. Molar mass = 12.01 + 2(32.07) = 76.15 g/mol. Do not use CO$_2$'s molar mass.

scoring_points.description: For the correct pressure from PV = nRT: 1 point

required_elements: $P = nRT/V = (0.1313\text{ mol})(0.08206\text{ L·atm/mol·K})(325\text{ K}) / 5.0\text{ L} \approx 0.70\text{ atm}$

wrong_examples: Using T in °C (325°C) — must use T in Kelvin (325 K). The problem already gives T in K.
Forgetting to divide by the volume — P = nRT/V, not nRT.
common_traps: Ideal gas law: PV = nRT. Solve for P = nRT/V. Temperature must be in Kelvin (given as 325 K already in this problem).
